paper.title: When Does Combining Language Models Help? A Co-Failure Ceiling on Routing, Voting, and Mixture-of-Agents Across 67 Frontier Models

research_directions.title: Co-Failure on Open-Ended Generative Tasks (beyond verifiable benchmarks)
research_directions.seed_question: Does the co-failure ceiling (β > 0) hold on tasks without verifiable answers: summarization, creative writing, long-form analysis, dialogue?
research_directions.motivation: The paper's strongest empirical claim (β caps orchestration) is currently limited to programmatically verifiable tasks. If the ceiling generalizes to open-ended generation, the practical implications are much wider.
research_directions.approach_suggestion: Develop reliable automated or human-in-the-loop grading for open-ended generation quality. Measure β across a panel of frontier models on diverse generative tasks. Use the content-controlled technique from §5 (same task in verifiable vs. open-ended format) to test transfer.
research_directions.payoff: Either confirms the ceiling is universal (massive practical implications) or identifies a boundary where open-ended generation diverges from verifiable tasks (sharpens the theory).
research_directions.leveraged_artifact: Prop. 1 ceiling identity, content-controlled methodology (Fig. 3), Clopper-Pearson certificate
research_directions.competition_risk: Low — the paper is the first to measure β at market scale on verifiable tasks; others may follow on generative
research_directions.dependency: Reliable automated quality judging for non-verifiable outputs

research_directions.title: The Common-Mode Atom: What Causes All Models to Fail Together?
research_directions.seed_question: What specific mechanisms drive the common-mode atom? Is it shared training data contamination, shared architecture biases, shared RLHF alignment, or shared safety filters?
research_directions.motivation: The paper identifies a common-mode atom but does not explain its source. Understanding the mechanism could inform model procurement (prefer uncorrelated failure modes) and training (explicitly target niche of non-co-failing queries).
research_directions.approach_suggestion: Ablation studies on model families from the same provider vs. cross-provider pairs. Analyze co-failure queries to identify patterns: are they ambiguous, adversarial, culturally specific, or critically dependent on certain knowledge? Trace to training data overlap. Test models before and after fine-tuning/RLAIF.
research_directions.payoff: Could enable a theory of model complementarity — which model combinations minimize β, not just ρ.
research_directions.leveraged_artifact: Common-mode atom analysis (Fig. 2, Prop. 2-3), 67-model full-Σ residual decomposition
research_directions.competition_risk: Moderate — several groups are studying model error correlations [15, 35]
research_directions.dependency: Access to training data provenance across providers

research_directions.title: Query-Level Routing Signal: What Makes a Router Work?
research_directions.seed_question: When the oracle gain G is large (realizability-bound regime), what signal — if any — can a router use to capture it? Are there query features that predict which model will be correct when the best model is wrong?
research_directions.motivation: The paper shows deployable routers capture almost none of G (9%, CI spans 0). This is arguably the most important open problem: the ceiling is open on science MC tasks, but no existing router can exploit it.
research_directions.approach_suggestion: Systematic exploration of routing architectures: embedding-space similarity to training examples, difficulty estimators (e.g. entropy, perplexity), model-specific confidence calibration, few-shot characterization of per-model error patterns. Use GPQA-Diamond (G=0.154) as the testbed. Compare against the cost-aware oracle frontier.
research_directions.payoff: If router signal exists, unlocks large accuracy gains on realizability-bound tasks. If it provably doesn't, the ceiling on multiple-choice tasks is architectural, not fundamental.
research_directions.leveraged_artifact: Pillar A routing results (Fig. 9), TF-IDF+domain, gradient-boosted, and LLM-as-router baselines
research_directions.competition_risk: High — routing is an active area [4, 31]; Dekoninck et al. [3] unify routing and cascading
research_directions.dependency: Logged prompts (not currently released for the market-scale matrix)

research_directions.title: Optimal Ensemble Design Under Cost-Aware Diversification
research_directions.seed_question: What is the optimal ensemble cardinality and composition across ρ levels at matched member quality, validated out-of-sample?
research_directions.motivation: The diversification limit k*(ρ, c) is derived (Prop. 6, 7) with sensitivity λ as a decision-rule Jacobian. But the empirical fit of λ across ρ levels and out-of-sample prediction of k* remains open. The matched-quality test rests on one provider-matched band.
research_directions.approach_suggestion: Scale the matched-quality experiment to multiple provider-matched bands across different ρ levels. Fit λ empirically using the Jacobian formula (Prop. 7). Predict k* out-of-sample on held-out bands. Test the cost-aware rule against the oracle frontier.
research_directions.payoff: Actionable guidance for practitioners: given a budget and set of models, what's the optimal ensemble?
research_directions.leveraged_artifact: Props. 5-7, the sensitivity λ derivation, matched-quality test methodology
research_directions.competition_risk: Low — few papers connect cost-aware diversification to LLM orchestration
research_directions.dependency: ≥3 seeds for matched-quality; multiple ρ-level bands

research_directions.title: Joint Routing-Cascade Optimality on the Frontier
research_directions.seed_question: Can the optimal cascade-routing policy of Dekoninck et al. [3] outperform the naive confidence cascade used in this paper, and by how much?
research_directions.motivation: The cascade result (Pillar C) is measured against a naive confidence cascade. The optimal policy conditions on both L and H confidences [14], which this paper did not log. The upper bound may be higher.
research_directions.approach_suggestion: Replicate the cascade experiment with full logging of both L and H confidences. Implement the optimal two-model deferral rule [14] and the optimal cascade-routing policy [3]. Compare against the cost-aware oracle frontier. Test across different (L, H) pairs varying a_H, a_L, price ratio.
research_directions.payoff: Either validates the existing cascade results as near-optimal or identifies a gap worth closing.
research_directions.leveraged_artifact: Prop. 8 cascade collapse identity, 5-fold held-out evaluation, cost-aware oracle frontier
research_directions.competition_risk: Moderate — [3] and [14] are actively pursuing this
research_directions.dependency: Full confidence logging for both L and H models

research_directions.title: The Format Effect: Why Does Open-Endedness Create Co-Failure?
research_directions.seed_question: What is the mechanism by which open-ended (free-response) format induces co-failure while multiple-choice suppresses it? Is it the reduced guessing floor, the broader output space, or something else?
research_directions.motivation: The content-controlled GPQA experiment (Fig. 3) shows that format alone flips the regime. Understanding why could inform evaluation design and model training.
research_directions.approach_suggestion: Systematic format ablation: vary number of MC options (2→5→10), add open-ended with hinted structure (fill-in-blank, single-word answer, short phrase). Test whether co-failure is continuous (appears gradually as options are removed) or discrete (threshold effect). Analyze whether it's a guessing-floor phenomenon (MC gives 1/n chance of random correct).
research_directions.payoff: Could separate co-failure into content-driven vs. format-driven components, enabling targeted mitigation.
research_directions.leveraged_artifact: GPQA content-controlled test (Fig. 3, App. G), regime classification (Fig. 4)
research_directions.competition_risk: Low — the format effect is uniquely demonstrated in this paper
research_directions.dependency: Additional format-ablated benchmarks across multiple domains

research_directions.title: The Calibration Trap: Systematic Impact of Pearson-vs-Tetrachoric in LLM Evaluation
research_directions.seed_question: How widespread is the Pearson-of-indicators calibration error across the broader LLM evaluation literature, and to what extent does it inflate reported diversity benefits?
research_directions.motivation: The paper documents that an earlier version of its own analysis fell into this trap, and that the naive Pearson calibration inflates the gap by an order of magnitude (32× vs. 2.5×). This is likely not unique to this paper.
research_directions.approach_suggestion: Survey papers reporting ensemble/fusion/routing gains. Recompute co-failure and diversity metrics using tetrachoric (latent) correlation vs. Pearson-of-indicators. Measure the magnitude of the artifact across benchmarks (MC, open-ended, code) and task families.
research_directions.payoff: Methodological cleanup that could materially change reported diversity results. High citation potential as a cautionary note.
research_directions.leveraged_artifact: Pearson vs. tetrachoric comparison (§5), the paper's own calibration correction
research_directions.competition_risk: Low — few researchers are aware of this specific trap
research_directions.dependency: Access to published outcome matrices or model responses

research_directions.title: Market Churn and the Option Value of Breadth
research_directions.seed_question: How does the option value of broad model access change under different churn regimes (fast frontier vs. plateau), and how should buyers dynamically allocate across epochs?
research_directions.motivation: The paper's secondary churn analysis (App. E) is observational and stylized. A proper dynamic allocation model with calibrated churn primitives could guide multi-year procurement strategy.
research_directions.approach_suggestion: Extend the real-options framework (Prop. 9) with empirically estimated churn rates ν and capability gap parameters (μ, η) from the 2024-2026 release history. Solve the full Bellman system linking the static allocation (App. A) to the dynamic option (App. E). Test sensitivity of the build-vs-route threshold.
research_directions.payoff: Operational guidance for enterprise LLM procurement: when to commit (self-host) vs. when to route (broad access).
research_directions.leveraged_artifact: Prop. 9, Cor. 2, churn measurement on the 2024-2026 timeline (Fig. 11)
research_directions.competition_risk: Low — the combination of churn economics + LLM orchestration is novel
research_directions.dependency: Sustained cost-quality tracking across future model releases

research_directions.title: Pre-Deployment Orchestration Audit Tooling
research_directions.seed_question: Can the Clopper-Pearson certificate (Prop. 1) be developed into a production-ready tool that enterprises use to decide whether to orchestrate, and if so, what adoption patterns emerge?
research_directions.motivation: The paper ships `beta_certificate.py` but it's a research tool. Productionizing it — with CI integration, cost-aware bounds, and domain-specific calibration — could substantially reduce wasted orchestration engineering.
research_directions.approach_suggestion: Build a library/tool: (1) input: small labeled sample (n=100-300) from target workload, (2) output: certified upper bound on orchestration gain over SOTA single model. Add cost-aware extension (compare bound against orchestration overhead of extra API calls / routing latency). Validate on enterprise production workloads.
research_directions.payoff: Direct practical impact. Every team evaluating orchestration should run this test.
research_directions.competition_risk: Low — the paper is first to propose this specific pre-deployment test
research_directions.dependency: Enterprise adoption; integration with API gateways

research_directions.title: Training Models to Not Co-Fail: Diversity by Design
research_directions.seed_question: Can models be explicitly trained (via data selection, architecture, or RL) to have complementary failure modes, i.e., to minimize β rather than maximizing individual accuracy?
research_directions.motivation: Current training maximizes individual model accuracy, which may inadvertently increase co-failure (if all models converge to the same correct answers and same failure patterns). Could a diversity-aware objective reduce β without sacrificing individual performance?
research_directions.approach_suggestion: Train variants of an open-weight model (e.g., Llama-3, DeepSeek) with objectives that penalize correlated errors against a reference model. Alternatively, use ensemble distillation with explicit diversity regularization. Measure β on MATH-500 and other open-ended tasks. Feasibility check: does β reduce faster than individual accuracy?
research_directions.payoff: If positive: a new axis for model improvement (failure diversity). If negative: confirms that co-failure is fundamental and unavoidable.
research_directions.leveraged_artifact: Prop. 2 (common-shock model prediction), common-mode atom framing
research_directions.competition_risk: Moderate — mixture-of-agents work [24, 37] touches on quality-diversity trade-off
research_directions.dependency: Access and compute to train/fine-tune models with diversity objectives

research_directions.title: Scalable Code-Failure Measurement with Official Judges
research_directions.seed_question: Does the co-failure signature (β=0.079, 3.1× tetrachoric underpricing) replicate at full 67-model scale with an official Codeforces/LiveCodeBench hidden-test judge?
research_directions.motivation: The code result is the only one with a CI excluding 1, but rests on 18 models and k=5 events. A precise magnitude at full scale is the one remaining sharpening the paper explicitly calls for.
research_directions.approach_suggestion: Run the full 67-model pool on a code benchmark suite with official hidden-test judge (e.g., LiveCodeBench, HumanEval-plus). Measure β, the Pearson/tetrachoric gap, and the full-Σ residual. If the signature holds at scale, it definitively establishes that co-failure generalizes beyond math.
research_directions.payoff: Definitive cross-domain replication; addresses the paper's own top limitation.
research_directions.leveraged_artifact: App. F code contest methodology, full-Σ residual decomposition pipeline
research_directions.competition_risk: Low — the paper explicitly calls for this, and its data/code are released
research_directions.dependency: Official judge access; execution sandbox for 67 models × problems

research_directions.title: Optimal Delegation Under Co-Failure: When to Stop Multi-Model Orchestration?
research_directions.seed_question: Given a measured β and a cost-aware diversification limit k*(ρ, c), what is the optimal multi-model orchestration policy across routing, voting, and cascading — and when should one just use the single best model?
research_directions.motivation: The paper identifies individual constraints (Prop. 1 ceiling, Prop. 6 k*, Prop. 8 cascade limit) but stops short of a unified multi-mode policy. Combining them into a single optimal policy is the natural endpoint.
research_directions.approach_suggestion: Formulate a joint optimization: given query distribution, model prices, qualities, and measured β, choose among (a) single-best, (b) cascade, (c) fusion, or (d) routing with the option to escalate. Solve the joint policy (Bellman over modes). Calibrate to the 67-model pool. Compare against each individual mode.
research_directions.payoff: The single algorithmic result that subsumes the paper's three pillars. 'Optimal orchestration in the co-failure era.'
research_directions.leveraged_artifact: Props. 1, 4, 6, 8; two-regime classification (Fig. 4); cost-aware oracle frontier
research_directions.competition_risk: Moderate — Dekoninck et al. [3] already unify routing and cascading
research_directions.dependency: Full logged prompts and confidences from all modes
